comments.text: I believe that this problem in [Er80,p.106] refers to the same problem: "1. Let $f (x)$ be the smallest integer so that there is a set of residues\[a_p \pmod{p}, p < f(x)\qquad\text{(1)}\]so that every integer $n < x$ satisfies one of the congruence (1). In particular must $f(x)$ be significantly larger than $x^{\frac12}$? Also it would be very useful to estimate $F(x)$ if we only require that the number of integers $n \le x$ not satisfying any of the congruences (1) is $o(x/\log x)$ (where $p < F(x)$). Is $F(x)$ significantly smaller than $f (x)$? These problems can of course be extended if more than one residue is omitted. It is not clear who first formulated this problem---probably many of us did it independently. I offer $\max(1000\text{ dollars}, \frac12 \text{ my total savings})$ for clearing up of this problem. It is clear that many important problems could be attacked if we would know a little more." Besides the additional reference, I wish to highlight the prize offered for the problem. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
